issues.summary: Phase 0 — Pre-work: Data Prep & Content Definition
issues.description: All foundational work that must be completed before writing a single line of Godot code. Includes exporting the agent directory to JSON, defining open content items (assistant name, company name, art direction), and establishing data formats.

issues.summary: Phase 1 — Godot 4 Prototype (Weeks 1–3)
issues.description: Build the 3D office world with first-person navigation, NPC proximity-dialogue, the agent data layer, assistant chat UI, and the unlock gate system. Critical path: M1 → M4 → M8. If you can walk through a lobby, approach an NPC, and see their agent info — Phase 1 is proven.

issues.summary: Phase 2 — I/O Bridge: Live Agent Output (Weeks 4–6)
issues.description: Connect the Godot game world to real agent CLI processes via a local Python WebSocket bridge. M8 is the proof-of-concept milestone: walk up to an NPC → type a question → real agent response appears in the dialogue panel. The game becomes the tool.

issues.summary: Phase 3 — Polish, All 9 Floors & Full Tutorial Arc
issues.description: Post-prototype expansion: all 9 department floors, full NPC set, NPC animations + pathfinding, complete Week 1 tutorial arc, real assistant API connection, streaming I/O visualization, art pass, ambient sound. Define specifics once M8 is working.

issues.summary: Define in-world assistant — name and personality
issues.description: The always-on navigation companion that appears as a chat overlay (not an agent NPC). Warm, slightly playful tone. Separate from the employee characters — this is the player's guide to the office world itself. Name and personality should feel intentional, not generic. Worth designing carefully before scripting any dialogue.

issues.summary: Define tutorial startup — company name and backstory
issues.description: The fictional startup the player has been building with the founder before Day 0. Should feel like a real startup — not a placeholder. Name, what the product does, how far along it is. This is the emotional context for why the new office matters. The company name and backstory will appear in Founder call dialogue (Day 0) and throughout the tutorial arc.

issues.summary: Export 137 agent .md files to agents.json
issues.description: Convert the full employee directory from .md format to a single agents.json file. Required before Milestone 3 (AgentRegistry autoload). Schema per agent: { name, role, dept, color, description, tools }. Verify all 137 entries parse correctly and the structure matches the planned AgentRegistry.gd loader. Source: adamtasteslikegood/claude-code-tresor.

issues.summary: Define art direction and rough visual moodboard
issues.description: Even a rough direction helps grey-boxing decisions. Questions to answer: interior style (modern tech-office? brutalist? warm indie?), lighting mood (warm and lived-in or cool and corporate?), color palette consistency with the 9 department color codes. One or two reference images / screenshots is enough to unblock M2.

issues.summary: M1 — Install Godot 4 + first-person controller (~1 hour)
issues.description: Download Godot 4 from godotengine.org (~80MB, no install needed). Open Asset Library inside Godot → search "First Person" → download the FPS demo. Hit Play and walk around. That working FPS controller is your foundation. Create a new project and copy the CharacterBody3D controller into it. Done when: player walks, looks around, has collision.

issues.summary: M2 — Grey-box the office layout (4–6 hours)
issues.description: Build the entire office space with plain CSGBox3D geometry before any textures or art. Rooms to block out: lobby/entrance, player's office, server room, engineering floor (open plan), war room/meeting room, 2–3 locked corridors. Add Area3D doorway triggers on each room entrance. Bake NavigationRegion3D for pathfinding. Done when: you can walk through the whole space and it feels right to navigate. Do NOT add art until this is validated.

issues.summary: M3 — Agent data layer: load employee directory as game data (~2 hours)
issues.description: Convert agents.json (from Phase 0) into the game's data layer. Create autoload/AgentRegistry.gd that loads agents.json at startup and returns agent data by ID. Create autoload/GameEvents.gd with core signals: npc_approached, npc_left, task_completed, floor_unlocked. Create autoload/GameState.gd with unlock tracking. Add all three to Project → Autoload. Done when: AgentRegistry.get_agent("systems-architect") returns correct data in-game.

issues.summary: M4 — First NPC + proximity dialogue (~3 hours) [CRITICAL PATH]
issues.description: The core loop. NPC scene: CharacterBody3D + Area3D proximity trigger + Label3D name tag. npc.gd reads from AgentRegistry by agent_id export var. When player enters proximity → GameEvents.npc_approached fires → HUD dialogue panel populates from agent data. First NPC: Systems Architect in the server room. Done when: walk up → panel shows agent name + role + description. Walk away → panel hides. This template is used for all future NPCs.

issues.summary: M5 — Assistant chat UI overlay (~4 hours)
issues.description: Always-on CanvasLayer chat panel (NOT an NPC). Toggle with Tab or tilde key. Contains: scrollable message history, LineEdit input, Send button. Returns scripted/hardcoded responses in Phase 1. This is the player's always-on companion separate from the agent NPCs — it handles navigation, schedule, and task check-ins. Phase 2 upgrade: swap scripted responses for HTTP/WebSocket calls to real API.

issues.summary: M6 — Unlock gate system + 2D building map (~5 hours)
issues.description: GameState.complete_task() checks unlock gate rules and calls unlock_floor(). Door triggers check GameState.is_unlocked(). 2D building map widget in HUD: rooms are colored rects using department colors, locked rooms grey, updates on GameEvents.floor_unlocked. Default unlocked: lobby + server room. First unlock gate: complete first sprint task → engineering floor unlocks. Done when: completing a mock task unlocks a door and the map updates.

issues.summary: M7 — Python WebSocket bridge (~1 day)
issues.description: A small Python process running alongside the game on the same machine. Godot sends task requests over local WebSocket (ws://localhost:8765); bridge spawns agent CLI subprocess, captures stdout, returns result as JSON. No cloud, no deployment, no auth. Install: pip install websockets. Test standalone with wscat before wiring into Godot. Then write ws_client.gd in Godot and test the full round-trip: Godot sends → bridge receives (log) → bridge returns → Godot receives (log).

issues.summary: M8 — First live agent output in-world [PROTOTYPE COMPLETE] (2–4 hours)
issues.description: Wire it all together: walk up to Systems Architect NPC → dialogue panel opens → player types a real question → ws_client.send_task() fires → bridge invokes @systems-architect via CLI → response JSON returns to Godot → output appears in dialogue panel → response also logged to inbox. This is the milestone where the game becomes the tool. Everything after this point is polish and expansion.

issues.summary: Phase 3 planning — define specifics once M8 is working
issues.description: Post-M8 expansion work. Rough scope: all 9 department floors fully blocked out and textured, full NPC set (all dept leads + core 8 agents), NPC idle animations + pathfinding, complete Day 0–5 tutorial arc with full dialogue scripts, real assistant API connection (replace scripted responses), streaming I/O output visualization, ambient sound design, art pass. Break this into sub-tasks after M8 is validated — no point defining granular tasks before the core loop works.

issues.summary: Godot 4 Game — Agent Knowledge Base Explorer
issues.description: A first-person 3D office game built in Godot 4 where players explore a 9-floor building and interact with NPCs representing AI agents. Milestones: Phase 0 (data prep), Phase 1 (3D prototype), Phase 2 (live agent I/O bridge), Phase 3 (polish + full tutorial arc). Critical path: M1 → M4 → M8 (first live agent output in-world). Migrated from TO-19–TO-35.

Migrated from TO-19

issues.summary: M1 — Godot setup
issues.description:
Phase 1 milestone — Godot prototype foundation.
Goal
Godot 4 installed, base project created, player character moves with collision.
Checklist
Download Godot 4Run First Person Shooter demo — confirm movement worksCreate new projectImport FPS CharacterBody3D controller into new projectConfirm player walks, looks around, has collisionBlockers
Open question #5 (web export vs desktop) should be resolved before starting — project settings differ.
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M2 — Grey-box office
issues.description:
Phase 1 milestone — blocked-out 3D space the player can walk through.
Goal
Player can walk through lobby → player office → server room → engineering floor → war room. Each room has a trigger, navigation is baked. Prototype feels spatially correct.
Checklist
Block out lobbyBlock out player officeBlock out server roomBlock out engineering floor (open plan)Block out war room / meeting roomAdd 2–3 locked corridors (future floors)Add Area3D doorway triggers on each room entranceBake NavigationRegion3D for the full floorPlaytest: does it feel right to walk through?Dependencies
M1 must be complete (player controller works)
Open question #3 (art direction) doesn't block grey-box but unblocks texturing later
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M3 — Agent data layer
issues.description:
Phase 1 milestone — data + state infrastructure.
Goal
Agent fleet loaded from JSON, registry queryable, game events + state tracking autoloaded. Foundation for NPC wiring and unlock logic.
Checklist
[ ] Create data/agents.json from directory
[ ] Create autoload/AgentRegistry.gd
[ ] Add AgentRegistry to Project → Autoload
[ ] Test: AgentRegistry.get_agent("systems-architect") returns correct data
[ ] Create autoload/GameEvents.gd with core signals
[ ] Create autoload/GameState.gd with unlock/task tracking
Dependencies
Pre-work: export 137 agent .md files → agents.json (tracked as separate Task)
Open question #7 (save game format) blocks GameState.gd
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M4 — First NPC
issues.description:
Phase 1 milestone — first interactive NPC in the server room.
Goal
Walk up to Systems Architect NPC → dialogue panel shows with agent info → walk away → panel hides.
Checklist
[ ] Create NPC scene (CharacterBody3D + Area3D + Label3D)
[ ] Write npc.gd with agent_id export + proximity signal
[ ] Place Systems Architect NPC in server room
[ ] Create basic HUD dialogue panel (CanvasLayer)
[ ] Wire GameEvents.npc_approached → dialogue panel populates from agent data
[ ] Playtest: walk up to NPC → panel shows → walk away → panel hides
Dependencies
M2 (server room exists)
M3 (AgentRegistry available)
Open question #12 (dialogue branching) may significantly affect scope
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M5 — Assistant chat UI
issues.description:
Phase 1 milestone — assistant overlay the player can toggle and chat with (scripted responses for now).
Goal
Tab or ~ opens chat overlay. Player types a message, gets a scripted response from the in-world assistant. Messages persist in scroll history.
Checklist
[ ] Create assistant overlay (CanvasLayer)
[ ] Build chat panel UI (message list + input + send button)
[ ] Implement toggle keypress (Tab or ~)
[ ] Add scripted responses for Day 0/1 context
[ ] Add basic message history (scroll container)
[ ] Playtest: toggle chat, send a message, get a response
Dependencies
Open question #1 (assistant character name) — blocks, needed for scripted responses
Scripted responses will be swapped for real agent API in M8
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M6 — Unlock + map system
issues.description:
Phase 1 milestone — progression mechanics.
Goal
Completing a task unlocks a door. The 2D building map widget updates to reflect new unlocked zones.
Checklist
Implement GameState.complete_task() with gate checksImplement GameState.unlock_floor() + is_unlocked()Wire door triggers to GameState.is_unlocked()Build 2D building map widget in HUDConnect GameEvents.floor_unlocked → map updates colorTest: complete a task → door unlocks → map updatesDependencies
M3 (GameState autoload exists)
M2 (doors/corridors to gate)
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M7 — Python bridge
issues.description:
Phase 2 milestone — WebSocket bridge between Godot and real agent CLI processes.
Goal
Godot can send a task to a Python WebSocket server → server spawns the right agent CLI as subprocess → response streams back to Godot.
Checklist
[ ] Install Python websockets: pip install websockets
[ ] Write bridge.py (WebSocket server + subprocess agent invocation)
[ ] Test bridge standalone: send it a task via wscat or websocat, verify agent CLI response returns
[ ] Write ws_client.gd in Godot
[ ] Test: Godot sends a task → bridge receives it (log to console)
[ ] Test: bridge returns a response → Godot receives and logs it
Risks
Subprocess management (concurrent tasks, zombies, timeouts) is load-bearing
Open question #11 (agent response latency UX) becomes urgent once this is working
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: M8 — First live agent output in-world
issues.description:
Phase 2 milestone — Prototype complete.
Goal
Walk up to NPC → type a real question → real agent response appears in-world. End-to-end vertical slice.
Checklist
[ ] Wire dialogue panel "send" button → ws_client.send_task()
[ ] Wire ws_client.response_received → dialogue panel updates with output
[ ] Wire response → also logs to inbox
[ ] Test end-to-end: walk up to NPC → type question → real agent response appears
[ ] MILESTONE: Prototype complete
Dependencies
M4 (NPC dialogue panel exists)
M7 (bridge works)
Open question #6 (agent error handling in-world) should be answered before this ships
Completion signal
This issue closing = prototype complete. Time to step back, play the build, and decide Phase 3 scope.
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: Phase 3 — Polish + expansion (backlog)
issues.description:
Backlog placeholder — specifics locked once M8 ships. Do not start work inside until prototype is complete.
Rough scope
World
All 9 department floors blocked out
Textures / basic materials applied
Lighting pass (day/night? warm office lighting?)
Ambient sound (HVAC hum, distant typing, coffee machine)
NPCs
All 9 department lead NPCs placed and scripted
Core 8 agents placed in server room
NPC animations (idle, talking, working)
NPC pathfinding (walk between rooms)
Tutorial
Full Day 0 cutscene (Founder call)
Day 1–5 scenes scripted and implemented
Unlock gates for all 9 departments
Tutorial completion state → free play
Assistant
Connect assistant chat to real API (not scripted)
Schedule view
Inbox with task history
Notification system
I/O visualization
Task status indicator while agent is running
Streaming output (show response as it arrives)
Error handling / failed task display
Multi-agent task orchestration visualization
Confluence
See PM Task Tracker

issues.summary: Content: in-world assistant — name and personality
issues.description:
Blocks M5 (assistant chat UI).
The in-world assistant is the player's primary ambient voice — needs a name and a personality that feels like a real startup tool, not a generic AI mascot.
Deliverables:
Name (single word preferred)
1-paragraph personality description: tone, vocabulary, what they do / don't do
5–10 sample utterances for Day 0 / Day 1 contexts
Scope: Non-technical — design + writing decision, not code.

issues.summary: Content: tutorial startup — company name
issues.description:
Blocks M4 dialogue scripts.
The fictional company the player is joining. Name sets the tone for every NPC line and every document the player sees in-world.
Deliverables:
Company name
1–2 sentence description (what do they do, what stage)
Founder name (used in Day 0 video call)
Scope: Writing decision. Should feel like a real early-stage startup, not a joke name.

issues.summary: Content: player character — name / customization options
issues.description:
How the player is identified in-world.
Deliverables:
Default name (if none set)
Customization options? (name, pronouns, cosmetic?) — or locked to a canonical character?
How is name used in dialogue? (NPCs address the player by name? Or only assistant does?)
Scope: Design decision with downstream impact on dialogue variable injection.

issues.summary: Content: Day 0 dialogue script — Founder video call
issues.description:
Full script for the Day 0 onboarding cutscene.
Context: Player just joined. Founder calls via video to brief them.
Deliverables:
Opening line
Core mission briefing (what is the company, what does the player do)
Tour teaser (what they'll unlock)
Handoff to assistant
Scope: ~150–250 word script + stage directions (camera, cuts, UI overlays).
Blocks: M4 (if Day 0 runs before first NPC) or Phase 3 tutorial polish (if Day 0 is saved for full tutorial cut).

issues.summary: Content: Day 1 dialogue scripts — Systems Architect, Security Auditor
issues.description:
First two NPC conversations the player experiences in the prototype.
Systems Architect (server room NPC — placed in M4):
Intro dialogue (when player approaches)
Scripted response to 2–3 common "what do you do?" queries
Handoff line when player completes first task
Security Auditor (second NPC):
Intro dialogue
Scripted response to 2–3 queries
Handoff line
Scope: ~100 words per NPC per dialogue branch. Tone consistent with company personality (depends on TO-45).

issues.summary: Content: visual / art direction
issues.description:
Even a rough moodboard helps grey-boxing decisions.
Deliverables:
8–12 reference images (or sketches) showing desired aesthetic
Short description: "It looks like ___ with ___ from ___"
Color palette (3–5 colors + accent)
Material vibe: clean/corporate? retrofuturist? cozy/warm?
UI visual language: flat/minimal? skeuomorphic? glassmorphic?
Scope: Doesn't block M1 or M2 grey-box, but blocks Phase 3 texturing and UI polish.

issues.summary: Content: sound direction
issues.description:
Ambient sound design direction.
Deliverables:
Ambient office sound layers (HVAC, distant typing, coffee machine, server hum, etc) — rough list
Music tone (none? ambient loops? dynamic stings on events?)
NPC voice handling (text-only? subtitled speech? synthesized voice?)
Scope: Direction doc, not actual asset production. Informs Phase 3 ambient sound work.

issues.summary: Data prep: export 137 agent .md files → agents.json
issues.description:
Pre-work for M3.
Walk the agent directory from the source repo, serialize every agent definition to a single JSON file.
Deliverables:
data/agents.json with 137 entries
Each entry: { id, name, role, dept, color, description, tools }
Validation script: python validate_agents.py reports any missing fields
Source: https://github.com/adamtasteslikegood/claude-code-tresor

issues.summary: Data prep: verify agents.json schema
issues.description:
Pre-work for M3.
Confirm the JSON schema matches what AgentRegistry.gd will expect.
Deliverables:
JSON Schema definition file (or doc) covering: name, role, dept, color, description, tools
Validator that runs against data/agents.json and passes cleanly
Flag any agent that has missing or non-conforming fields
Depends on TO-52 (the JSON file must exist).

issues.summary: Data prep: define scene / dialogue data format
issues.description:
Decide how dialogue scripts are authored and loaded.
Options:
JSON files (easy to edit externally, weak typing)
GDScript Resources (strong typing, requires Godot editor to edit)
Ink / other dialogue DSL (excellent for branching, adds a dependency)
Deliverables:
Decision + example file of the chosen format
Document for writers on how to add new dialogue
Blocks: M4 dialogue scripting if the format needs to be in place before authoring.

issues.summary: Data prep: define player profile save format
issues.description:
How GameState persists across sessions.
Tied to Open Question #7.
Options:
JSON file on disk (portable, inspectable)
Godot Resource (.tres) (type-safe, harder to debug externally)
Neither — in-memory only for prototype, save support deferred to Phase 3
Deliverables:
Decision + example save file
Load / save implementation in GameState.gd
Error handling for corrupt / missing save
Blocks: M3 (GameState.gd can ship without save, but full M3 requires this decision).

issues.summary: Decide: web export vs desktop
issues.description:
Open question #5 — blocks M1.
Godot supports both targets, but project settings and some plugin compatibility differ.
Options:
Web (HTML5 export): Frictionless share link, weaker performance, limited FS access, no subprocess (kills the live agent bridge)
Desktop (Win/Mac/Linux): Full capability, higher distribution friction, requires installer
Recommendation: Desktop. The WebSocket bridge + Python subprocess in M7 is incompatible with a pure web export. Web can come later as a demo-only read-only version.
Deliverable: Decision logged. Godot project settings aligned. Export template installed.

issues.summary: Decide: save game format
issues.description:
Open question #7 — blocks M3 (full GameState.gd).
Options:
JSON file (human-readable, easy to debug externally, no type safety)
Godot Resource .tres (type-safe, editor-friendly, opaque externally)
Don't implement save for prototype — defer to Phase 3
Linked to data prep issue TO-55.
Deliverable: Decision + one-line rationale.

issues.summary: Decide: how to handle agent errors in-world
issues.description:
Open question #6 — blocks M8.
Real agent calls will fail sometimes: timeout, API error, subprocess crash. How should the game surface that?
Options:
NPC says "I'm unavailable right now" (diegetic, nice but hides info)
Error toast / message in inbox (out-of-world, clear)
Visual effect on the NPC (glitch, sparks) + diegetic message
Silent retry with spinner
Deliverable: Decision + UX mockup or description.
Closely related to Open Question #11 (latency UX).

issues.summary: Decide: platform target (Win / Mac / Linux)
issues.description:
Open question #8 — informs Phase 3 polish.
Godot exports all three, but test time multiplies per platform. Pick primary for first playtest cohort.
Context: Adam's dev machine is Linux. Most indie playtesters are Win/Mac.
Options:
Linux-first (dev parity, fast feedback, tiny audience)
Mac-first (common dev playtester base)
Windows-first (largest gaming audience)
All three from day one
Deliverable: Primary platform + secondary platforms + when secondaries enter the build/test loop.

issues.summary: Decide: multiplayer scope
issues.description:
Open question #4 — affects Phase 3+.
Single-user for the prototype is locked. But does the post-prototype vision include multiplayer?
Options:
Never (single-player, narrative-driven game)
Async (shared office, async chat, persistent world state)
Live co-op (multiple players in the same office simultaneously)
Why now: Even deferred, the answer informs Save format (TO-55), GameState.gd (TO-38), and data architecture decisions in M3. Knowing "never" simplifies; knowing "maybe" means keep options open.
Deliverable: Direction + one-line rationale, even if "deferred pending playtest".

issues.summary: Decide: agent response latency UX
issues.description:
Bonus decision surfaced during import — becomes urgent at M7.
Real agent CLI calls take 5–90+ seconds. How does the game handle that wait?
Options:
"Thinking" animation on the NPC (classic, safe)
Loading bar / spinner in dialogue panel
Time passes visibly in the game world (clock ticks, ambient NPCs move) — most immersive, most work
Player can walk away and come back when done (async notifications)
Deliverable: UX pattern picked + scope for implementation. Can ship M8 with a simple spinner then upgrade in Phase 3.
Related: TO-58 (error handling UX).

issues.summary: Decide: dialogue branching model
issues.description:
Bonus decision surfaced during import — significantly affects M4 scope.
Are NPC conversations:
Multi-turn, open-input: Player types freeform queries → agent responds. Maximum fidelity, matches the real-agent bridge in M8, but harder to script.
Single-shot, menu-driven: Player picks from 3–5 options → agent responds. Classic RPG model, easier to script, but loses the "real agent" conceit.
Hybrid: Scripted openings with canned options, then transitions to freeform when the player engages the assistant overlay.
Why this matters: The dialogue data format (TO-54) depends on this.
Deliverable: Model chosen + example for one NPC.

issues.summary: Decide: in-game time model
issues.description:
Bonus decision surfaced during import.
Does one "day" in-game = one play session? Or does time pass while idle?
Options:
Session = day: Save/load discrete units. Linear progression. Simple.
Real-time persistent: World state advances between sessions. Complex, atmospheric.
Task-driven: Time advances when tasks complete, not on wall clock. Good match for the agent-based gameplay loop.
Affects: Tutorial pacing (is Day 0 → Day 5 linear or spread out?), save format (TO-55), assistant notification model.
Deliverable: Model chosen + implication for M6 unlock logic.
